title: Yoshiyuki Sadamoto Illustrations for Sega Saturn
display_title: Yoshiyuki Sadamoto Illustrations for Sega Saturn
wikitext:
[]: Yoshiyuki Sadamoto Illustrations for Sega Saturn             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Sep 27, 2010 10:39 am Hey kids, so I picked up a copy of Yoshiyuki Sadamoto Illustrations for Sega Saturn recently. It's more or less an interactive version of Der Mond for the Sega Saturn. It includes (with a few exceptions) all of the same artwork, from all of the same works. It isn't anything to rant or rave about, especially if you have a copy of Der Mond, but it is noteworthy for having video commentary by Sadamoto. I've captured the video, and while it's untranslated, perhaps someone can oblige. http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wnXorrDAmEw

[]:             Quote Post by InstrumentalityOne » Mon Sep 27, 2010 11:21 am Neat. Did you run it on your PC or something? Edit: I stand corrected: IT´S SUPER NEAT.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Sep 27, 2010 11:31 am The Sega Saturn is remarkably easy to play import games on. All you need is an Action Replay card, and BAM, you're ready to roll. No soldering, no modding, no obnoxious disc swapping, just a special memory unit that loads the software at system boot.

[]:             Quote Post by InstrumentalityOne » Mon Sep 27, 2010 11:46 am The Eva Monkey wrote: The Sega Saturn is remarkably easy to play import games on. All you need is an Action Replay card, and BAM, you're ready to roll. No soldering, no modding, no obnoxious disc swapping, just a special memory unit that loads the software at system boot.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Sep 27, 2010 12:03 pm InstrumentalityOne wrote: Could you run it on your PC with an emulator?

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Mon Sep 27, 2010 12:09 pm Sega Saturns are one of the few systems that can't be emulated. Which explains why there's very little information on the Second Impression game with Mayumi. At least the first first Sega Saturn game was later re-released as an interactive DVD so people know about that... I'll take a look at the youtube vid somepoint today, it sounds neat.

[]:             Quote Post by InstrumentalityOne » Mon Sep 27, 2010 12:17 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: you just enjoy the hobby of collecting oddities.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Sep 27, 2010 12:41 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Sega Saturns are one of the few systems that can't be emulated.

[]:             Quote Post by Hawq » Mon Sep 27, 2010 1:54 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Sega Saturns are one of the few systems that can't be emulated.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Mon Sep 27, 2010 11:48 pm I've talked to a few people who tried various emulators for their copy or a ROM, tried it myself with the Yaba-emulator/others, and Second Impression at least seemed impossible to emulate. Now, I have seen a few low-quality clips of Second Impression (and Sega Saturn NGE 1) on Youtube and Nico in the past; perhaps they were video-recorded OR emulated. But thanks for the tip, I'll check out the Japanese one. And yes, I'll watch the youtube Sadamoto thing this week. >_>

[]:             Quote Post by soul.assassin » Tue Sep 28, 2010 1:03 am There might be a way, as I used to extract video clips from Playstation discs. If your PC could read off the disc, you should be able to access the directory. Now, one of the video formats used by the Saturn is FILM/CPK (Cinepak) and Total Video Converter claims it can handle this type of format. Sega FILM/CPK file format Description: FILM is a multimedia container file format developed by Sega for use on its early CD-ROM home video game consoles. Based on analysis of a number of Sega CD and Saturn games, it appears that the format was first developed sometime during the era of the Sega CD, the first CD-based video game console. There are many early variations of the FILM format observed on a number of Sega CD titles. Further, early FILM files have been observed on at least one 3DO game (Lemmings). The Sega Saturn video game console, released in 1995, was also CD-ROM-based and apparently offered developers a standardized SDK for full motion video (FMV) playback using a modified variant of the well-known Cinepak video codec. It is possible to store both audio and video in a FILM file. Alternatively, the format is able to handle either video without audio, or vice versa.

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Tue Sep 28, 2010 10:11 am Since youtube uses ffmpeg based conversion engines there's a chance that it could convert them directly, if I'm not wrong ffmpeg can handle the FILM format (less conversion steps will mean less loss of quality). Anyway there were games with proprietary formats like 2nd Impression that can't be converted. Anyway thanks a lot for that video evamonkey, your capture is actually of an excellent quality.

[]:             Quote Post by AchtungAffen » Wed Sep 29, 2010 3:11 pm Hmm, I have the rom for this shit... never ran it though, but it's available through the mule.

[]:             Quote Post by InstrumentalityOne » Wed Sep 29, 2010 3:17 pm AchtungAffen wrote: the mule

[]:             Quote Post by AchtungAffen » Wed Sep 29, 2010 3:37 pm Of course I can. There are certain measures you can take to avoid getting false results on searches (like filters and shit), but it's a common sense thing to me. I wish I could use Linux and Amule, but I'm still stuck with Windows and common olde emule. While torrent might be faster and shit, links and files live a lot longer on the ed2k network, and there's stuff there which was posted and shared from the days before torrent sites and still live for download. The good thing about the mule is that that's what the Chinese, master pirates, use. Anything posted on Tokyo3CN was available through emule, for ex.
